text: The surgeon performs a vagal sparing maneuver by identifying the vagal trunk and accessing the lesser sac between the vagal trunk and the stomach. The gastric pouch is tailored using an Endo-GIA stapler inserted through the right upper quadrant port, rotated clockwise for anterior lip formation. A calibration tube is used, and the stapler is then inserted through the left mid quadrant port aiming towards the left crus area. An SFGL retractor is placed, and vertical staplers are applied from the left upper quadrant port at Palmer's point, with buttressed staplers used to complete the pouch.

The surgeon splits the omentum and identifies the ligament of Treitz, measuring approximately seventy-five to one hundred centimeters to form the omega loop. A Penrose drain is applied to prevent twisting of the omega loop, which is lifted next to the gastric pouch area. The surgeon begins tailoring the gastrojejunostomy (GJ) by placing an anterior outer stitch using a 22 cm Vicryl 2-0 suture, followed by a posterior outer layer stitch, ensuring blood supply by avoiding the staple line with each bite.

The surgeon locks the last stitch and transects the omega loop using a white load from the left mid quadrant port. Two or three bites are taken to split the mesentery slightly, facilitating easier JJ reconstruction. The edictube is advanced, and a gastrotomy is performed approximately 1.5 centimeters using an ultrasonic dissector. An enterotomy is then dilated, and the posterior inner layer is started with a 22-centimeter suture.

The surgeon takes bites through all layers from the bowel side, tightening each stitch until reaching the corner. Corner stitches are emphasized for preventing leaks. The surgeon then proceeds with the anterior inner layer using a 17-centimeter suture, carefully avoiding the posterior wall while dunking the mucosa and passing the edict tube. The anterior inner layer is completed by hiding the inner row of sutures and tightening them after each bite.

The surgeon ties the stitch to the previously locked posterior outer layer and performs a leak test under saline immersion. The Roux limb is counted and sutured to the PP-limb using a 25 cm Ethibond suture. A silk suture is placed for the assistant. The orientation of the Roux limb is checked from the JJ area back to the GJ area to ensure the mesentery is not twisted. Enterotomies are made approximately one centimeter away from the stitch in a diagonal fashion and spread longitudinally along the bowel axis. Using a wide 60 mm Endo-GIA from the right upper quadrant port, the common enterotomy is closed while exiting to prevent widening. The common enterotomy is closed with a 20 cm 2-0 Vicryl suture in a running fashion, starting with initial bites at the common channel ruling area and holding the Ethibond suture near the crotch to invert it inward.

The surgeon inverts the tissue inwards and ties it to the Ethibond tail, then proceeds with closing the mesenteric defect. The closure is performed by piercing the base of the mesentery and tightening the suture to ensure the common channel and Roux limb connection is not narrowed at the jejunojejunostomy (JJ).
The surgeon closes the Peterson defect using two figure-of-eight sutures, ensuring the defect is securely closed.
